title: Shutterstock AI Search
text: Shutterstock AI Search is a conversational, generative AI tool that lets you find stock assets by describing your creative vision in natural language rather than typing strict keywords. It delivers cross media results, suggesting relevant images, videos, and music at the same time so a campaign stays cohesive. This is the multimodal discovery work I focus on at Shutterstock, bringing my retrieval and ranking background to a generative search experience.

title: Reveal
text: Reveal is a single page application I built during OpenSearchCon to demonstrate the three pillars of Generative Discovery: Intent, Context, and Cognition. The idea is that search finds but discovery reveals. Intent is the user query, analyzed and encoded as a semantic vector that captures meaning, context, and purpose rather than just a bag of words. Context is a session vector that remembers the conversation and is recency weighted, so the system carries what came before. Cognition is the reasoning step: it decomposes contradictory queries, filters candidates, then applies an LLM rerank. Reveal is an education and demo piece that makes those pillars concrete against a live agentic query workflow.

title: Reveal
text: Reveal is built framework free. Retrieval runs on OpenSearch with a hybrid of BM25 over text and kNN over embeddings, so the Intent vector and the recency weighted Context vector both resolve against the same index. Generation, query decomposition, and the LLM rerank in the Cognition step use the OpenAI SDK pointed at NVIDIA NIM as the primary model, with gpt-4o-mini as the fallback. There is no LangChain or LlamaIndex in the path. The app is a Next.js single page application served on Vercel. Keeping the moving parts few makes the pillars easy to explain and the demo predictable.

title: Reveal
text: On Reveal I own the system end to end: the Intent and Context vector design, the embedding choice and index mapping, the Cognition reasoning path, and the serving function. My specific contribution is the relevance layer. I brought the learning to rank experience from classical IR into a modern multimodal setting, treating the final LLM rerank as a ranking problem over candidates rather than a single opaque generation. That framing is what makes discovery feel principled instead of arbitrary, and it is the through line from my classical IR background to generative discovery.

title: Reveal
text: The hardest decision on Reveal was whether to use a heavy orchestration framework or to stay framework free with direct SDK tool calling. The pull toward a framework was real: it promises graph based control flow and prebuilt retrievers. I chose to stay framework free because the control flow behind Intent, Context, and Cognition was small enough that the SDK tool API covered every case, and because each framework layer adds a debugging surface and a version treadmill that I would own forever. The senior signal here is knowing that query decomposition and rerank were the things I actually needed, and that I did not need a framework to get them. The cost was writing the routing and the eval harness by hand. The benefit is a demo I can reason about and explain completely.

title: Reveal
text: As a conference demo, Reveal makes Generative Discovery legible: a viewer can watch a query become an Intent vector, see Context accumulate across a session, and follow Cognition as it decomposes a contradictory query, filters, and reranks. The grounded approach also means the system explains its picks, pointing back to the signals that surfaced each result. It was built for education and demonstration at OpenSearchCon rather than as a production service, and the remaining work is deepening each pillar and tightening the low confidence behavior so the system stays honest when it is unsure rather than guessing.

title: Scene Finder
text: Scene Finder is multimodal scene search across video and stills. You describe a scene in natural language, for example a wide shot at dusk with two people walking, and it retrieves the matching moments from a library of footage and images. It exists because timecode and filename search do not match how people remember footage. People remember content and mood, so the search has to operate over what is actually in the frame.

title: Scene Finder
text: Scene Finder samples frames from video, embeds them with a CLIP class image and text model, and indexes the vectors in OpenSearch with kNN enabled. Queries are embedded with the same model and matched by vector similarity, with a lexical fallback over any available captions. The design choice that matters most is the sampling rate: too sparse and you miss brief moments, too dense and the index and cost balloon. The retrieval and serving stay framework free, consistent with the rest of the portfolio.

title: Scene Finder
text: The hardest decision on Scene Finder was the frame sampling strategy. A fixed interval is simple but wastes vectors on static footage and misses fast cuts. I chose an adaptive sampler that keys frames on visual change, so dense action gets more samples and a held shot gets fewer. This kept the index size reasonable without losing the brief moments that users actually search for. The reasoning is the same relevance instinct from ranking work: spend resolution where the signal is.

title: GridWatch
text: GridWatch is anomaly detection and ranking over operational telemetry. Rather than firing an alert for every threshold breach, it ranks anomalies by how unusual and how actionable they are, so an on call engineer sees the few signals that matter first. It treats alerting as a ranking problem, which is a direct application of learning to rank to observability.

title: GridWatch
text: The hardest decision on GridWatch was how aggressively to suppress alerts. Suppress too much and you hide a real incident, suppress too little and you recreate the noise the tool was meant to fix. I chose to rank rather than filter: nothing is hidden, but the ordering pushes low value alerts down. That preserves recall while still giving the operator a clean top of list, and it makes the suppression auditable because every alert is still present and explainable by its rank.

title: OpenClaw
text: OpenClaw is open retrieval tooling for grounded question answering over a defined corpus. It is the reusable core behind the grounded answers in this portfolio: tool routing over distinct retrieval functions, a guardrail that refuses out of scope questions, and a hand rolled grounding eval. The point of OpenClaw is to show that you can build trustworthy retrieval without a heavyweight framework.

title: OpenClaw
text: The hardest decision on OpenClaw was where to put the guardrail. The naive place is after retrieval, but that wastes a search call and risks answering from weakly related chunks. I run a relevance gate before retrieval and a low confidence gate after it. The pre check refuses anything not about the subject with a single fixed line, and the post check refuses when the top retrieval score falls below a threshold. Two gates, one fixed refusal, no fabrication. The out of scope behavior is the highest value part of the system because it is what makes the grounded answers trustworthy.

title: Selective search
text: Selective search reduces the cost of search over massive collections with limited computing resources. Most search engine frameworks split a large collection into subunits called shards to use distributed computing, and at query time they search every shard for every query, which is expensive at scale. This project investigates partitioning a collection into shards of similar documents and then searching across only a few relevant shards per query instead of all of them, without hurting search quality. Dividing the collection by document content similarity is called topic based partitioning, and searching only a few relevant shards for a given query is called selective search, following the research by Kulkarni et al.

title: Selective search
text: The payoff of selective search is lower search cost at comparable quality. Because documents are grouped into topic based shards, a query only needs to touch the few shards likely to hold relevant documents rather than the entire collection, which cuts the work per query in an environment with a large dataset and low compute. This is the same relevance instinct that runs through my other work: spend the compute where the signal is, and prove the quality holds with evaluation rather than assuming it. There is a recorded presentation of this work.

title: Am I replaceable by AI
text: Am I replaceable by AI is a small, playful calculator that estimates how replaceable a given job is by AI. It is an educational and entertainment app, built to make the abstract question of AI job impact tangible and a little fun. It is deliberately lightweight, the opposite end of the spectrum from the production retrieval systems, and a way to keep building and shipping small things.

title: Dessert near me
text: Dessert near me is a fun app that helps you find desserts nearby. It is an entertainment project rather than a research one, a light break from the retrieval and ranking work, and there is a short recorded demo of it in action.

title: Generative Discovery retrieval at 2000+ RPS
text: The 2000+ RPS retrieval system is the Generative Discovery infrastructure I architect in my current role as Staff Software Engineer, AI at Shutterstock. It powers RAG style generative AI applications by combining dense vector retrieval, hybrid search, and metadata grounding at more than 2000 requests per second across hundreds of nodes. Building it meant leading the Solr to OpenSearch migration that redesigned the retrieval layer for cloud native scale and enabled ML powered ranking pipelines, and continuously improving the ranking pipeline that retrieves and reranks the best candidates using learning to rank, bi encoders, and cross encoders. Retrieval architecture optimization drove a 25 percent latency and cost reduction while relevance metrics improved, decoupling scale from quality tradeoffs. Retrieval quality is held accountable by offline and online evaluation frameworks built on precision and recall, relevance judgments, and human in the loop feedback.

title: Career summary
text: I am a Staff and Principal level AI engineer based in NYC, with more than seventeen years of experience spanning classical information retrieval and modern AI. Most recently I have spent about four years at Shutterstock building multimodal discovery. Before Shutterstock I spent roughly thirteen years total: about six years as a professional services and consulting engineer working directly with customers to deliver search and AI systems, and before that about seven years in software engineering. My path runs from relevance ranking, model training, and learning to rank on production search systems, into agentic and multimodal systems that span retrieval, ranking, and image generation. The through line across every role is the same question: how do you put the right information in front of the model or the person at the right moment. I describe my work as the line between retrieval and intelligence.

title: Education
text: I hold a Master of Science in Computer Science from San Francisco State University and a Bachelor of Engineering from Visvesvaraya Technological University. The graduate work in computer science is where my information retrieval and search foundations were formed, and it connects directly to the relevance ranking and retrieval systems that run through my career.

title: Classical IR and relevance ranking
text: For much of my early career I worked on search relevance and ranking: tuning analyzers and query parsing, building learning to rank pipelines, running offline relevance evaluation, and operating retrieval at production scale. This is where the habits formed that still define how I work: measure relevance with a held out judgment set, treat latency as a first class constraint, and never ship a ranking change without an eval that shows it helped. Those habits now underpin the retrieval systems I build at Shutterstock.

title: Agentic and multimodal systems
text: My current work at Shutterstock, over the past four years, is building multimodal discovery and agentic systems: retrieval augmented generation with real tool routing, multimodal search over images and video, and image generation pipelines. The centerpiece is Generative Discovery infrastructure combining dense vector retrieval, hybrid search, and metadata grounding at more than 2000 requests per second across hundreds of nodes. I keep these systems framework free, using the model SDK tool API directly rather than an orchestration framework, and I keep evaluation hand rolled and reusable. The classical IR background and my years of customer facing consulting are the advantage here, because grounding, relevance, and understanding what a user actually needs are exactly the problems that decide whether one of these systems is trustworthy.

title: Generative Discovery on OpenSearch: Intent, Context, Cognition
text: At OpenSearchCon India 2026 I give a talk titled Generative Discovery on OpenSearch: Intent, Context, Cognition. The premise is that search finds but discovery reveals, and at Shutterstock, serving one of the world's largest licensed creative libraries at 2000+ requests per second, that difference is everything. Generative Discovery changes the contract: it treats every interaction as a signal of intent, builds context across modalities, and applies cognition to surface what users did not know to ask for. The talk covers three principles. Intent means unifying multimodal signals into rich representations. Context means session aware RAG pipelines built on OpenSearch. Cognition means generative agents that treat OpenSearch as an intelligent reasoning substrate rather than a passive index. It draws on production decisions and lessons from building generative discovery systems.

title: From Boolean Search to Agentic Generative Discovery
text: In From Boolean Search to Agentic Generative Discovery: Orchestrating 25 Years of IR Innovation, I trace how modern agentic systems fold classical information retrieval into contemporary AI. Drawing on building search infrastructure for Shutterstock's media library, I show how agents decide when to use BM25 for exact matches, embeddings for semantic recall, cross encoders for precision, and CLIP for visual search. The talk covers lexical search foundations, the evolution to multimodal retrieval, hybrid fusion strategies, and practical agent orchestration patterns that combine keyword and semantic approaches. A recorded version is on YouTube, and the companion demo is the Intent, Context, Cognition app.

title: Writing and posts on LinkedIn
text: My writing lives on LinkedIn as short notes and longer posts on retrieval, ranking, agentic systems, and keeping the stack framework free. A recurring theme is that for a static or slowly changing corpus the SDK tool API is sufficient and an orchestration framework is mostly liability: another dependency, another debugging surface, another version treadmill. Framework free is a deliberate position for the cases where routing and evaluation are simple enough to own directly, not a rejection of frameworks everywhere. The latest activity and articles are on my LinkedIn recent activity feed.

title: Courses and continuing education
text: I have completed more than forty DeepLearning.AI courses spanning agentic systems, voice, and generative AI, alongside large language models, retrieval augmented generation, transformers, LLMOps, and foundations in machine learning and information retrieval. Rather than list every course, the summary is the shape of the study: deep on retrieval and ranking, broad on the modern LLM stack, and hands on with the providers I actually build against. The full certificate list is on my LinkedIn certifications page; the resume carries one summarizing line.

title: Certifications
text: My earned certification is AWS GenAI Professional, alongside 40+ DeepLearning.AI courses concentrated in agentic AI, voice, and generative AI. I am currently progressing toward two NVIDIA credentials, NCP-AAI and NCA-AIIO, in agentic AI and AI infrastructure; those are in progress rather than earned. The mix reflects a deliberate focus on agentic AI and the infrastructure to serve it. The full certificate list is on my LinkedIn certifications page.

title: Running
text: Running is my happy place. I am always up for a marathon or the next challenge. Nothing beats the runner's high, a clear mind, and the feeling of crossing the finish line. Reward me with a cold beer and a slice or three of pizza, and that is pretty much a perfect day. You can follow me on Strava.

title: Hiking
text: Weekends on the trail with family and friends are my favorite way to recharge. The combination of fresh air, mountain views, cool breezes, and peaceful trails surrounded by trees and water brings joy and connection, and keeps the oxytocin flowing. You can follow me on AllTrails.

title: AI for Autism
text: AI for Autism is where my passion and personal journey meet. As the mother of an autistic son, I have spent years learning, advocating, and supporting his growth in communication and confidence. This journey inspires me to explore how AI can provide practical, evidence informed support for autistic children and their families. It also shapes how I think about access and design in everything else I build, because a system that only works for the median user is not actually finished. I would be happy to connect and share experiences, and I share more on my YouTube channel.
